ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roti prata with egg and hollandaise topping. Mapped to flatbread food product as the closest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ (Flat bread) used as the closest defensible code for prata-based dish. No composite code exists for prata with egg, turkey ham and hollandaise in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 200 g, comprising one plain prata base (about 100 g) topped with one poached egg, two to three slices of turkey ham, and a generous drizzle of hollandaise sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Prata (poached egg, turkey ham, hollandaise) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite dish (cr_id F05020093). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are used as-is. Saturated fat estimated at 8.0 g/100 g based on hollandaise sauce (butter-based) and ghee content consistent with HPB contextual notes; this field is not explicitly reported in the HPB record and is documented as an estimate.

gi_source.title: Roti (Indian flatbread) — Glycemic Index entry; analogous estimate applied to plain prata base
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for prata with poached egg, turkey ham and hollandaise. GI of 70 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain roti / Indian flatbread (GI ~70, high category), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this dish. The egg, turkey ham and hollandaise sauce contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (70) × carbohydrate per serving (22.4 g/100 g × 200 g serving = 44.8 g) / 100 = 31.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 452 mg/100 g from processed turkey ham and hollandaise sauce; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 904 mg sodium, exceeding 40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request hollandaise sauce on the side, and choose a reduced portion of turkey ham to keep per-meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined prata base with GI ~70 and 22.4 g carbohydrate/100 g drives rapid postprandial glucose rise; saturated fat ~8.0 g/100 g from hollandaise and ghee further impairs insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this dish to one piece of prata per meal, request a minimal drizzle of hollandaise sauce, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8.0 g/100 g) from butter-based hollandaise and ghee raises LDL cholesterol; high sodium (452 mg/100 g) elevates blood pressure; processed turkey ham contributes sodium nitrates associated with cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly, substitute hollandaise with a lemon-herb dressing to reduce saturated fat, and limit turkey ham to one slice to reduce sodium and nitrate exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.0 g/100 g from hollandaise (butter) and ghee exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; sodium nitrates in processed turkey ham may worsen tissue inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish and replace hollandaise sauce with a lemon-herb or olive-oil-based dressing to reduce pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prata with Poached Egg, Turkey Ham and Hollandaise Sauce is a Singaporean fusion breakfast dish that reimagines the classic Western Eggs Benedict by substituting the traditional English muffin with a plain roti prata — a South Indian-origin flaky flatbread that is pan-fried in ghee or oil until golden and layered. The prata base is topped with one or two poached eggs, two to three slices of processed turkey ham, and a generous drizzle of butter-based hollandaise sauce. The result is a rich, indulgent dish with a crispy-chewy base, silky egg yolk, savoury cured meat, and a buttery, lemony sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 337 kcal/100 g, with high fat (22.7 g/100 g, of which approximately 8.0 g is saturated fat from hollandaise and ghee), moderate protein (10.8 g/100 g from egg and turkey ham), and moderate refined carbohydrate (22.4 g/100 g) from the prata base. Dietary fibre is low at 1.0 g/100 g. Sodium is high at 452 mg/100 g, driven by processed turkey ham and hollandaise sauce. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), consistent with refined Indian flatbread. A 200 g serving delivers approximately 674 kcal, 904 mg sodium, and a glycaemic load of about 31. This dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. It is commonly served at Singaporean cafes and kopitiam-style eateries as a weekend brunch or breakfast item.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020093, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated at 8.0 g/100 g based on the known composition of hollandaise sauce and ghee, consistent with HPB contextual notes; this is documented as an estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain roti/Indian flatbread as the closest approved comparator.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.0 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from the known butter and ghee content of hollandaise sauce and prata preparation; it is not explicitly reported in the HPB record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean fusion dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain roti/Indian flatbread as the closest approved comparator; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 4. Halal status is marked false because hollandaise sauce typically contains butter (dairy) and the dish as served in cafes may not be prepared under certified halal conditions; turkey ham halal certification varies by vendor. 5. Allergens include gluten (prata dough), eggs (poached egg and hollandaise), milk (butter in hollandaise and ghee in prata), and soy (possible in turkey ham processing).
